# Amn

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Nation | **Capital:** Athkatla | **Region:** Lands of Intrigue

---

## Overview

Amn — the Merchant's Domain — is a tremendously wealthy nation in West Faerûn. Its population (historically ~3 million, mostly human with a large halfling minority) and its control of key trade routes to Calimshan, Baldur's Gate, and colonial ports in the Moonshaes and Chult make it one of the most important trading centers in Faerûn. Its capital, Athkatla, is known throughout the Realms as the "City of Coin."

---

| **Capital** | Athkatla |
| **Population** | ~2,963,520 (1372 DR) |
| **Races** | Humans 83%, Halflings 15%, Half-orcs 1%, Other 1% |
| **Religions** | Bane, Chauntea, Cyric, Selûne, Sune, Waukeen |
| **Imports** | Magic items, mercenaries, pearls, siege weaponry |
| **Exports** | Ale, armor, beer, gems, grain, horses, iron, jewelry, weapons |
| **Currency** | 1pp = 1 roldon = 5 gold danters = 10 electrum centaurs = 100 silver tarans = 1000 copper fandars |

# Ascore

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Ruins | **Status:** Destroyed | **Region:** Anauroch desert edge

---

## Overview

Ascore was once a wealthy and bustling port city of the dwarven nation of Delzoun during the Netherese Empire — a gateway where dwarves traded with elves of Eaerlann and Myth Drannor and humans of Netheril. By the 14th century DR, it had become a lonely, windswept ruin on the edge of Anauroch, near the Frozen Sea, reputedly haunted by ancient evil.

The docks that once met the Narrow Sea now meet only the sea of sand that has half-buried the city.

---

| **Current Region** | Frozen Sea edge, Anauroch |
| **Type** | Ruins (formerly Port City) |
| **Current Inhabitants** | Dune stalkers, hags, Shadovar |
| **Former Inhabitants** | Dwarves, humans, elves |

---

body:
# Baldur's Gate

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Metropolis | **Faction:** Spree Alliance | **Status:** Fallen | **Region:** Sword Coast



---

## Overview

Baldur's Gate — also called simply "the Gate" — is the largest metropolis on the Sword Coast and among the most prosperous and influential merchant cities in Faerûn. A crowded city of commerce and opportunity, it once maintained its neutrality through wealth and the strength of its peacekeeping forces: the Watch and the powerful Flaming Fists mercenary company.

That neutrality is gone. The leaders of Baldur's Gate are now members of the Spree Alliance. The city's incredible wealth and trade networks are now directed by the Spree.

---

## Geography

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Location** | South of Waterdeep, north of Amn; on the Coast Way road |
| **River** | On the north bank of the River Chionthar, ~40 miles east of the Sea of Swords |
| **Harbor** | Gray Harbor — natural bay |
| **Layout** | Upper City (flat); Lower City (built over steep bluffs overlooking Gray Harbor) |
| **Size** | Metropolis |
| **Population** | ~125,000 (1492 DR); ~120,000–140,000 (1479 DR) |

---

## Society

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Races** | Mostly human |
| **Demonym** | Baldurian |
| **Religion** | Outlawed (historically tolerant; current Spree policy bans organized worship) |

---

## Commerce

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Imports** | Stone |
| **Exports** | Dyes, fish, lamp oil, trade-coinage |

---

## Politics

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Government** | Plutocracy |
| **Executive** | Council of Four |
| **Legislative** | Parliament of Peers |
| **Affiliation** | Spree Alliance (formerly Lords' Alliance) |

---

## Campaign Notes

> [!warning] DM ONLY
> **Status (1508 DR):** Baldur's Gate joining the Spree Alliance is one of the major geopolitical blows of the campaign. The city's enormous wealth and trade network now fuel the Spree's operations across the Sword Coast.
>
> The Flaming Fists — previously a stabilizing peacekeeping force — are now effectively a Spree military asset. Whether they are true believers or reluctant enforcers is a potential story thread.
>
> The party has not visited Baldur's Gate. It exists primarily as a data point about how far the Spree's influence has spread southward.

---

## Related Notes

- [[Waterdeep]] — Northern ally; the great holdout against the Spree
- [[The Spree Alliance]] — Current controlling faction
- [[Lord's Alliance]] — Former affiliation; now effectively dissolved in the Gate

body:
# Candlekeep

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Fortress-Library | **Faction:** Neutral | **Status:** Active | **Region:** Sword Coast

---

## Overview

Candlekeep is the famous library-fortress that has stood as a bastion of enlightenment and knowledge on the Sword Coast for centuries. It houses the greatest collection of books and scrolls of lore in all of Faerûn. The keep is home to the Avowed — a group of scholars devoted to the great seer Alaundo and his collection of prophecies.

To enter the Inner Ward and access the Great Library, visitors (called "Seekers") must pass the magically-protected Emerald Door. Beneath the keep lie secured spellcasting chambers, catacombs, sewers, and subterranean passageways, including the Chamber of Lost Lore — a room allowing communion with long-deceased scholars.

---

## Description

A spectacular sight: scores of towers of varying heights, a skyline rising high above the coast within formidable 50 ft (15 m) walls of stone and iron. Its enormous double gates are forged from black metal, each bearing the sigil of Candlekeep — a castle with flames atop each tower.

Beyond the imposing front gates is the **Court of Air** — a vast cobblestone courtyard for visitors and Seekers. Beyond the Emerald Door lies the **Inner Ward** with the Great Library and the Avowed's personal quarters.

---

## Geography

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Region** | Sword Coast, Western Heartlands |
| **Aliases** | Citadel of Lies (derogatory), Castle of Tomes |
| **Imports** | Books |
| **Exports** | Knowledge, Books, Spellbooks |
| **Religions** | Oghma, Milil, Deneir, Gond |

---

## Society

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Population** | ~300 Avowed (1492 DR) |
| **Government** | Meritocracy |
| **Ruler** | Keeper of Tomes (Janussi in 1492 DR) |

---

## Campaign Notes

> [!warning] DM ONLY
> Candlekeep maintains strict neutrality and is not Spree-aligned. It may be one of the few truly safe repositories of knowledge still accessible to the party.
>
> **Possible use:** If the party needs information about the Nightwalker, Aurgolarossa, the Mantle Theory, or the Undying Watch, Candlekeep's library is the place. Getting in requires bringing a book of exceptional value as entry payment.
>
> The Avowed have prophecies from Alaundo — some of which may be relevant to the campaign's tragic resolution.

---

## Related Notes

- [[Lord's Alliance]] — Ally
- [[Waterdeep]] — Nearby major power

# Citadel Adbar

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Dwarven Fortress | **Faction:** Lords' Alliance | **Region:** Silver Marches

---

## Overview

Citadel Adbar is a massive dwarven fortress named for King Adbar, who began its construction in −370 DR and completed it in −272 DR — the last shard of the once-mighty dwarven kingdom of Delzoun. The Citadel provides surface access to miles of defensive granite corridors, tunnels, and hallways winding under the Ice Mountains. It can house up to 60,000 dwarves in relative comfort, though less than a third of that number are usually present.

# Citadel Felbarr

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Dwarven Citadel | **Faction:** Lords' Alliance | **Region:** Silver Marches

---

## Overview

Citadel Felbarr is a dwarven citadel at the base of the Rauvin Mountains along the River Redrun. Originally a fortress of 25,000+ dwarves, it has changed hands multiple times between dwarves, humans, and orcs throughout history. Since 1367 DR, it has been back in dwarven hands. It connects via the Silverymoon Pass to the south and Sundabar Vale to the east, with tunnels in the Upper Underdark connecting it to Mithral Hall to the west.

---

## Notable Locations

| Location | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **The Hammer** | First gate — two 40-foot-high stone gates |
| **The Anvil** | Second gate — two 30-foot-high stone gates at the river crossing |
| **The Runegate** | Pair of 20-foot-square stone doors covered in 32 deadly runes; the citadel's greatest accomplishment |
| **North & South Vigil** | Defensive areas with catapults and ballistae |
| **Hall of Ceremony** | High cavern with king's throne on raised dais |
| **Cemetery** | Most revered area; resting place of Clan Warcrown; always under expansion |

---

## Overview

Cormanthor is the great forest of the ancient elven empire of Cormanthyr — a large, dense woodland with trees growing over 100 ft (30 m) in the deep interior. # Cormyr

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Nation | **Faction:** Lords' Alliance | **Capital:** Suzail | **Region:** Interior Faerûn

---

## Overview

Cormyr — the Forest Country, Land of the Purple Dragon — is one of Faerûn's most historically prosperous nations. Protected by the elite Purple Dragons (military) and War Wizards (arcane corps), Cormyr has survived countless threats: the Goblin War, the death of King Azoun IV, Shadovar attacks, Zhentarim infiltration, and more. As of 1486 DR, Queen Raedra Obarskyr rules and focuses on rebuilding after years of war.

Adventuring requires an official license, but Cormyr's overstretched military means skilled help is always welcome — historically rewarded with land grants.

---

# Daggerford

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Small Town | **Faction:** Lords' Alliance | **Region:** Delimbiyr Vale, Sword Coast

---

## Overview

Daggerford is a small but stubborn town on the Trade Way in Delimbiyr Vale — the largest settlement between Waterdeep (150 miles north) and Baldur's Gate to the south. It is primarily a farming community but has grand ambitions to rival Waterdeep. A relic of the old kingdom of Delimbiyr, it refuses to fall. Its coat-of-arms is a silver dagger covered in blood on a field of dark blue.

---

## Overview

Everlund — formerly Besander's Blockhouse — is one of the greatest supporters of the Silver Marches league and the second-largest city in the region. Located southeast of Silverymoon on the north bank of the River Rauvin, it is one of the most active mercantile communities in the North. Resettled by humans from Ascalhorn in 515 DR, it grew from a fortified trading outpost into one of the North's most important trading cities.

# Forest of Tethir

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Forest | **Aliases:** Wealdath | **Region:** Northwest Tethyr

---

## Overview

The Forest of Tethir — known to elves as Wealdath ("unspoiled woods") — is the largest forest in the Lands of Intrigue, one of the largest in all Faerûn. Named after the epic elven hero Tethir, first elven dragonslayer. Located in northwest Tethyr just north of the Starspire Mountains, extending to the Dragon's Neck Peninsula. Some scholars consider it "the single most important thing" that shaped Tethyrian culture.

The forest is dark, cool, and rain-heavy — full of small lakes, pools, streams, and cascades. The Trade Way passes through the western portion near the coast.

---

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Ancient Dwarven City | **Faction:** Lords' Alliance | **Region:** Sword Coast North / Northdark

---

## Overview

Gauntlgrym was the ancient capital of the dwarven nation of Delzoun — a city of massive scale, with doorways large enough for dragons to pass through. Its location was lost for centuries, believed to be north of the River Dessarin. It was actually farther southwest. The city was rediscovered in 1357 DR.

The city is adorned with icons of the Morndinsamman and dwarven heroes. Doorways were constructed from iron, mithral, and stone — designed to open only for those of Delzoun blood, both magically sealed and tremendously heavy.

body:
# Hellgate Keep

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Ruins | **Status:** Sealed | **Region:** Northeast edge of the High Forest

---

## Overview

Hellgate Keep — originally founded as **Ascalhorn** — was an ancient elven city on the northeast edge of the High Forest, overtaken by demonic forces and eventually destroyed in 1369 DR. In the years following, its ruins became known as Hellgate Dell.

The site is now sealed by treants who maintain walls of sharp brier and thorn 30 ft (9 m) tall, stretching 50 ft (15 m) of land — blocking all entry. The ruins contain both wild magic zones and dead-magic zones.

---

## Geography

The keep lies along Skull Creek, which feeds into the Delimbiyr River. Key features of the ruins:

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Razorthorn Rift** | A wide rift along the southern cliff outcrop created when the Gatekeeper's Crystal destroyed the keep |
| **Grintharke's Gulch** | A 600 ft × 600 ft, 400 ft-deep inverted-pyramid gorge — center of the destruction |
| **Hellgate Dell** | 70 ft debris crag 2 miles south; overgrown with moss; ringed by 25 oak trees (5 are treants) |
| **Magic Pillar** | A thin magical column of water rising hundreds of feet on the western gulch slope — once a public well from Ascalhorn's time |

**Magical Hazards:** All flying magic (spells, potions, items) does not function in this area.

---

## History

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| Ancient | Founded as Ascalhorn — elven city |
| Medieval | Overtaken by demons and transformed into Hellgate Keep |
| 1369 DR | Destroyed — Gatekeeper's Crystal triggered massive explosion |
| After 1369 | Ruins sealed by treants; now known as Hellgate Dell |

---

## Campaign Notes

> [!warning] DM ONLY
> **Chapter 5 Destination.** The ruins of Hellgate Keep are the likely destination for the next chapter arc. The treants who sealed the ruins have been pulling back — something is breaking through from inside.
>
> The connection to Hellgate Keep was seeded in the High Forest arc. The dead-magic zones mean the party cannot rely on flight or certain spells inside.
>
> The demonic taint from the original Hellgate occupation may still be active in the ruins — or something new has moved in.
>
> **Key Detail:** The Grintharke's Gulch dead-magic zones are nested between wild magic zones — meaning failed spells might trigger wild surges rather than simply fizzling.

---

## Related Notes

- [[High Forest]] — The ruins sit at the northeast edge of the forest
- [[Treants / Turlang's Wood]] — The treant guardians who sealed the ruins are beginning to retreat

name: Helm'S Hold
body:
# Helm's Hold



## Quick Info
| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Region** | Sword Coast North — ~20 miles SE of Neverwinter |
| **Type** | City — Small Fortified City |
| **Controlling Faction** | [[The Spree Alliance]] — Merrick (Valas Halfinas) |
| **Status** | Occupied — Spree HQ |
| **First Visited** | TBD |
| **Access** | Open — all are welcome, but religious worship is prohibited |

---

## Description (Public)
Helm's Hold has stood for generations as a place of watchfulness and protection — a fortified city built around the faith of Helm, the Watcher. Founded by the Company of Crazed Venturers and anchored by the grand Cathedral of Helm, it was for centuries one of the most welcoming places in the North: a safe haven for travelers, a refuge for the desperate, a fortress of quiet devotion.

That is no longer what it is.

Merrick — the man rumored to lead the Spree Alliance — took Helm's Hold by force. He stormed the city, displaced its elected Chief Speaker, and made it the Spree's capitol and base of operations. The Cathedral of Helm still stands at the city's center, but open worship of Helm — or any deity — is now strictly prohibited.

By all reports, the city remains prosperous. Trade continues. The streets are kept. Anyone is welcome, so long as they do not pray.

---

## Description (DM Only)
> [!warning] DM ONLY
> - **The deep irony**: Helm's Hold is where Valas Halfinas was raised after Severinus Acustes rescued him from the streets of Eigersstor. He served in Helm's church here, rose through its ranks, and eventually vanished from this city on his way to adventure. He has now returned as Merrick — the head of an organization that has desecrated everything he once served. He banned religion here not out of mere ideology but out of personal devastation. This is the place that shaped him. He destroyed it on purpose.
> - **Severinus Acustes** — The mortal identity of Helm — was likely a figure at Helm's Hold during Valas's time here. Whether Severinus is still connected to the city in 1508 DR is TBD, but the location is deeply tied to that relationship.
> - **Amaris Vendanii connection**: Amaris grew up at House Invincible in Silverymoon, not Helm's Hold — but the Church of Helm's structure means both are part of the same religious institution. The desecration of Helm's Hold is the desecration of Amaris's faith's heartland.
> - **The Sanatorium**: The underground asylum beneath Helm's Cathedral has a history of being used for dark purposes — it was used to experiment on plague victims by corrupt clergy. The Spree likely uses it now. What is happening in the Sanatorium in 1508 DR is TBD but should be disturbing.
> - **The Crypts of the Vigilant Eye**: The labyrinthine crypts predate the Hold itself. They are a network of tunnels, burial chambers, and presumably now Spree-controlled underground spaces. Key to any infiltration or escape scenario.
> - **Heartward Hall** (temple of Sune) has been desecrated by the Spree. This is visible evidence of what Merrick's prohibition actually looks like in practice.
> - If the party ever comes to Helm's Hold, they are walking into the Spree's home territory — heavily defended, deeply ideological, and profoundly personal to Valas.

---

## Key Landmarks

**Religious Sites (Current/Former)**
- **Cathedral of Helm** — The grand cathedral at the city's center, around which Helm's Hold formed. Still standing. No longer a functioning temple. What it is now under Spree control is unclear — possibly repurposed, possibly sealed, possibly something worse.
- **Heartward Hall** — A temple of Sune formed from a marketplace shrine. Has been desecrated by the Spree Alliance. Status unknown beyond that.
- **The Sanatorium** — The underground asylum immediately beneath Helm's Cathedral. A history of dark use by corrupt clergy. Almost certainly in Spree hands.

**Underground**
- **Crypts of the Vigilant Eye** — A labyrinthine network of crypts that predates the Hold itself. Extends beneath the city in unknown directions. A potential infiltration route — or a trap.
- **The Warrens** — An incomplete drainage system under Scar Alley. Connects to the broader underground network.

**Civic Landmarks**
- **Heartward** — The city's central marketplace. Still functioning under Spree governance.
- **Town Hall** — Former council chambers, once an inn. Now presumably a Spree administrative center.

**Inns & Taverns**
- **The Hungry Flame** — A run-down tavern historically serving the city's more desperate residents. Likely still operating.
- **Venturer's Rest** — Another battered inn, once known as the Old Dirty Dwarf.

---

## Current State
Helm's Hold is the Spree Alliance's capital. It is run efficiently — Merrick governs it pragmatically, keeping trade flowing and citizens fed. The prohibition on religious worship is enforced but the city is not a warzone. It is more insidious than that: a functional city that has had its soul removed.

The Cathedral stands silent. Heartward Hall is desecrated. The Crypts are occupied by something the Spree has put there. And somewhere in the Sanatorium beneath the cathedral, experiments continue.

---

## History
Helm's Hold was founded by the Company of Crazed Venturers under First Guardian Dumal Erard — a position passed by martial combat. It grew into a small city centered on Helm's Cathedral, becoming a place of watchfulness, protection, and faith. Even through the Time of Troubles, when Helm ceased showing signs to his faithful, the city's clergy kept the doors open.

The city was governed for generations by an elected City Council of Speakers, led by a Chief Speaker. It survived internal corruption in the 1470s DR, when the Ashmadai planted a shapeshifting succubus in its leadership. Adventurers from Neverwinter helped cleanse it.

Its last Chief Speaker before the Spree's arrival was Amarandine Wanderfoot. She was displaced when Merrick took the city by force and made it his capitol. No record exists of what happened to her after.

---

## Key NPCs Present
- **Merrick** ([[Valas Halfinas]]) — The Spree's leader; governs from here; his connection to this place is deeper than anyone knows
- Amarandine Wanderfoot — Former Chief Speaker; fate unknown
- TBD — Spree lieutenants and garrison; Shadowblade and Shadowborn rank operatives
- TBD — Surviving clergy or citizens who remain under Spree rule

---

## Connected Factions
- [[The Spree Alliance]] — Controls and governs; established HQ here
- Church of Helm — Original founders; effectively destroyed here
- Church of Sune — Heartward Hall desecrated

---

## Connected Events
- Merrick's conquest of Helm's Hold — established the Spree's capital
- Valas Halfinas's history here — grew up, served, and vanished from this city; returned as its conqueror

---

## Sessions Visited
- TBD

---

## Map Reference
*See `Helm's Hold Map.md` and `Helm's Hold Map - Ink.svg` in World Reference for layout.*

---

## Rumors & Mysteries
- What exactly is happening in the Sanatorium? Locals whisper that sounds come from beneath the Cathedral at night.
- The Crypts of the Vigilant Eye go deeper than anyone mapped. Where do they come out?
- Merrick has been seen standing in the sealed Cathedral, alone, for hours. What does a man who banned religion do inside a cathedral?
- Some travelers say Helm's Hold is actually safer under Merrick than it was before — no crime, no corruption, everything running smoothly. How much of this is true, and how much is propaganda?
- What happened to Chief Speaker Wanderfoot?

---

## DM Notes
> [!note] Private
> - Helm's Hold is one of the campaign's most emotionally loaded locations. Every inch of it is personal to Valas — the place where he was rescued, raised, and believed. Playing it as simply "Spree HQ" misses the point.
> - If the party comes here, they are walking into Valas's inner sanctum. Consider what it looks like: did he preserve the Cathedral's architecture while stripping it of meaning? Did he leave Helm's iconography up as a twisted trophy? Did he destroy it?
> - The Sanatorium is the location most likely to contain active horror. Keep it ambiguous until needed.
> - The Crypts are the party's best infiltration route if they ever need to get in or out without going through the front gate.
> - Merrick standing in the Cathedral alone is a haunting image. If the party ever learns about this detail, it should make Valas feel like more than a villain.

### Geography Quick Reference
| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| **Location** | ~20 miles SE of Neverwinter, near Neverwinter Wood |
| **Type** | Small fortified city |
| **Government** | Spree Alliance (Merrick); formerly elected City Council |
| **Religious status** | All worship prohibited |
| **Notable underground** | Crypts of the Vigilant Eye, the Sanatorium, the Warrens |

name: High Forest
body:
# High Forest



## Quick Info
| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Region** | Northwest Faerûn — Savage Frontier |
| **Type** | Wilderness — Ancient Forest |
| **Controlling Faction** | [[Fey Alliance]] (nominally) — with active Spree and unknown threats |
| **Status** | Active — Contested and dangerous |
| **First Visited** | TBD |
| **Access** | Restricted — anyone who enters uninvited is seldom seen again |

---

## Description (Public)
The High Forest is a remnant of the ancient world, when all of Faerûn was covered in green and elves, giants, and dragons ruled. It is the greatest forest in Faerûn — up to 500 miles across, covering nearly 20% of the Savage Frontier. Bounded by the Nether Mountains to the north, the High Moor to the south, the Evermoors to the west, and the vast desert of Anauroch to the east.

What lies within the forest's depths is largely a mystery. Few venture there willingly, and those who do without permission seldom return. The forest is ancient and self-sufficient, and it does not welcome outsiders.

Since the Spree's invasion of the North, the Fey Alliance has dramatically expanded its presence in the High Forest. King Oberon now claims governance of the region, and fey patrols enforce a strict no-entry policy for non-residents. Anyone who enters without permission is considered a threat.

**Current State (1508 DR):** The High Forest is the active theater for Chapter 4. [[Augrin Bravecarver]] went missing somewhere in this forest. [[Bryleth]] is operating here as a Spree agent. Something in the forest is wrong — the Fey Alliance's presence is heavier than it should be, and the usual inhabitants (centaurs, treants, druids) are harder to find.

---

## Description (DM Only)
> [!warning] DM ONLY
> - **Augrin's situation**: The specific circumstances of Augrin's disappearance are TBD — flesh this out before the session. He may have been captured, led astray by Bryleth's manipulation of the forest, or stumbled onto something he wasn't supposed to find.
> - **Bryleth's operations**: She is using the High Forest as her operational base. The forest itself is her weapon — she has been manipulating root systems, animal behavior, and terrain to make the forest hostile to intruders. She knows the party is coming.
> - **The Nightwalker**: The two-phase boss fight referenced in the Campaign Dashboard is likely tied to the High Forest arc. A Nightwalker is a Greater Undead native to the Shadowfell — its presence here suggests a connection to Aurgolarossa's work on the Shadowfell, or to the Spree's experiments in the Dire Wood.
> - **The Fey Alliance's true interest**: The Fey Alliance isn't just "controlling" the High Forest — they originally established their hidden enclave here after displacement from the Feywild. The forest has deep fey resonance that drew them. They are protective of it in ways that go beyond politics.
> - **Hellgate Keep** (Chapter 5 hook): The ruins at the northeast tip of the forest are sealed by treants. Something in the ruins is connected to Chapter 5 — "Southwood / Hellgate Keep" is listed as the next chapter destination. Begin seeding this before the Nightwalker fight.
> - **The Endless Caverns** in the Star Mounts are said to connect to the Underdark and are home to dragons. Relevant to Aurgolarossa's history in the Thunder Peaks (she had a lair in a similar cave system).

---

## Key Landmarks

- **The Star Mounts** — An impressive mountain range at the center of the forest. Contains the Endless Caverns, said to be home to dragons and passages to the Underdark. The Star Mounts provide headwaters for both the Unicorn Run and the Heartblood River.

- **The Grandfather Tree** — An ancient arakhor (elder treant form) standing over 350 feet tall. Located deep in the heart of the forest. Surrounded by burial mounds of long-dead Uthgardt barbarians of the Tree Ghost tribe. A place of profound natural power.

- **The Dire Wood** — A region of forest within the wide bend of the Heartblood River. A great massacre once took place here. Strange supernatural phenomena originate from this location — it was once home to two liches both named Wulgreth. Still considered deeply cursed and avoided even by the forest's regular inhabitants.

- **Hellgate Keep (Ruins / Hellgate Dell)** — On the northeast tip of the forest. An ancient elven city called Ascalhorn, overtaken by demonic forces and destroyed in 1369 DR. Now a ruin known as Hellgate Dell, sealed off by treant guardians with 30-foot walls of brier and thorn. Contains wild magic zones and dead-magic zones. **Chapter 5 destination.**

- **Turlang's Wood** — The northern portion of the forest, where a band of treants under Turlang have been expanding the forest's reach. Their efforts originally sealed off Hellgate Keep from further incursion.

- **The Unicorn Run** — A sacred river flowing from the Star Mounts to the Delimbiyr. Considered so sacred that nature deities sometimes personally visit it in animal form. Elves, korreds, and halflings have strong taboos about fouling it.

- **The Heartblood River** — Flows through the eastern regions of the forest.

---

## Current State
The High Forest is the party's current operating theater. [[Augrin Bravecarver]] is missing somewhere within its borders — recovering him is the immediate objective. [[Bryleth]] is actively working against the party within the forest.

The Fey Alliance controls access to the forest in name but cannot fully patrol its vast interior. The deep forest is effectively ungoverned — home to ancient things that were old before the Fey Alliance was displaced from the Feywild, and before the Spree came north.

The forest is hostile by default. Animals behave strangely. Paths shift. Those who know what to listen for say the trees remember being older than the gods.

---

## History
The High Forest is one of the oldest places in Faerûn — a remnant of the time when all of the continent was covered in growth. It was once part of the great elven empire, and its depths still contain ruins of that age. Since then it has been home to centaurs, treants, dragons, fey, druids, gnolls, orcs, and more — rarely at peace, but somehow persisting.

The destruction of Hellgate Keep in 1369 DR was a significant event — a demonic-corrupted city at the forest's edge destroyed, its ruins subsequently sealed by treants. The Fey Alliance established their first enclave here after displacement from the Feywild, finding the forest's deep fey resonance a comfort. They have since expanded their control.

---

## Key NPCs Present
- [[Augrin Bravecarver]] — Missing; current rescue target; exact location TBD
- [[Bryleth]] — Active Spree operative; using the forest as a weapon
- Turlang — Ancient treant; guardian of the forest's northern reaches; potential information source
- TBD — Fey Alliance patrols and envoys

---

## Connected Factions
- [[Fey Alliance]] — Nominally governs; enforces no-entry policy
- [[The Spree Alliance]] — Active operations via Bryleth
- Druids of Tall Trees — Secretive order protecting the forest
- Church of Mielikki (Walkers of the Forest Way) — Historically present
- Emerald Enclave — Rangers and nature wardens

---

## Connected Events
- Augrin Bravecarver's disappearance — current crisis
- The Spree's northern invasion — brought greater Fey Alliance control to the forest
- Destruction of Hellgate Keep (1369 DR) — sealed ruins on the northeast edge

---

## Sessions Visited
- TBD — Chapter 4

---

## Map Reference
*See High Forest map in `zz_ Assets`. Star Mounts in center; Hellgate Keep ruins to northeast.*

---

## Rumors & Mysteries
- Something in the Dire Wood has been stirring. The supernatural phenomena are getting worse.
- The treants guarding Hellgate Keep have begun to pull back. Something is pushing through the briers.
- The Fey Alliance's patrols have been finding things in the forest that shouldn't be there — things from the Shadowfell.
- The Grandfather Tree has been quiet in a way it hasn't been for centuries. Older druids say it's listening for something.

---

## DM Notes
> [!note] Private
> - The High Forest is Chapter 4's main adventure environment. It should feel ancient, hostile, and layered — multiple factions with conflicting interests, and a forest that has its own agenda.
> - Bryleth's presence means travel through the forest is never straightforward. Paths close, animals attack, terrain shifts. She knows the party is here.
> - The Nightwalker fight is the chapter's boss encounter — set it up gradually. Darkness in places it shouldn't be. Cold spots. Animals fleeing in specific directions.
> - Hellgate Keep ruins are Chapter 5's destination — begin planting the hook before the chapter ends. Treants who used to seal the ruins have started to retreat. Something has broken through from inside.
> - The Fey Alliance's presence here gives the party potential allies — but Oberon and Titania will want something in return for help finding Augrin.

### Geography Quick Reference
| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| **Size** | ~500 miles across |
| **Borders** | Nether Mtns (N), High Moor (S), Evermoors (W), Anauroch (E) |
| **Government** | King Oberon (Fey Alliance) |
| **Population** | ~31,000 |
| **Religion** | Corellon, Chauntea, Eilistraee, Eldath, Mielikki, Silvanus |
| **Imports** | Armor, weapons, food |
| **Exports** | Ancient artifacts |

# High Ice

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Glacier | **Status:** Desolate/Dangerous | **Region:** North of Anauroch

---

## Overview

The High Ice is a desolate, monster-infested glacier north of the Anauroch desert, extended beyond its natural size by phaerimm magic. Its western edges give way to the Frozen Sea, a frozen desert where part of the ancient Narrow Sea once stood.

Despite being logical lair territory for frost giants, no true giant has entered the High Ice since a tribe attempted to enslave the local crystal dragons and was massacred.

---

## Inhabitants

Glacier snakes, ice jacks, ice toads, ice trolls, mammoths, remorhaz, snow cloakers, snow snakes, white puddings, winter wolves, yetis. Snow elves rumored to inhabit the deepest areas.

---

## Rumors & Legends

- Ancient Netherese ruins lay preserved within the ice, protected by restless spirits (including the sunken city of Runlatha, too deep to access)
- Augaurath, an ancient white dragon, is rumored to be worshiped as a god by yetis, winter wolves, and remorhaz
- The northernmost part of the glacier may be a massive white pudding hundreds of feet in diameter

---

## Related Notes

- [[Ascore]] — Ancient ruins on the edge of the High Ice region
- [[Icewind Dale]] — Region to the west

# Hillsfar

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Metropolis | **Region:** Moonsea | **Status:** Active — Lord's Alliance member

---

## Description (Public)
Hillsfar is a powerful city-state located on the southern coast of the Moonsea. Heavily involved in regional trade, it acts as a commercial hub for the surrounding cities and settlements. The city is known for its fine craftsmanship — particularly fine cloth, faceted gems, and its distinctive dragon's breath liquor — and for its nearly exclusively human population. Non-humans face significant discrimination and worse in Hillsfar.

The city has historically been notable for the Great Arena, where gladiatorial combat entertains the populace, and for the strict enforcement of anti-non-human policies that have defined its reputation across the Moonsea.

---

## Description (DM Only)
> [!warning] DM ONLY
> - Hillsfar's extreme anti-non-human stance makes it a source of tension with Lord's Alliance members who don't share those values.
> - The Moonsea is distant from the current campaign theater but relevant as a region — Hillsfar represents the less idealistic end of the Lord's Alliance.
> - The city's proximity to Myth Drannor and the Moonsea's general instability make it a potential future destination.

# Icewind Dale

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Arctic Region | **Capital:** Bryn Shander | **Region:** Frozenfar — Northernmost explored region in Faerûn

---

## Overview

Icewind Dale is an arctic tundra in the Frozenfar — the northernmost explored region in all of Faerûn. It earned its name from the harsh winds and icy storms that scour the landscape. Near-uninhabitable, with long nights and temperatures regularly below freezing, it is home only to the most hardened frontiersmen, pioneers, and barbarians. Beyond the scattered settlements dwell terrifying monsters of the North.

---

## Ten Towns

The only permanent settlements are the **Ten Towns** — a confederation that cooperates for mutual survival. Hub: **Bryn Shander**.

| Town | Location |
|------|----------|
| Bryn Shander | The mercantile hub; "Tenth Town" |
| Good Mead, Dougan's Hole | Redwaters lake |
| Easthaven, Caer-Konig, Caer-Dineval | Lac Dinneshere |
| Lonelywood, Bremen, Termalaine, Targos (walled) | Maer Dualdon |

---

| **Lac Dinneshere** | Waters shift color with changing weather |
| **Redwaters** | Named for a bloody ancient battle |
| **Kelvin's Cairn** | Highest peak; provides fresh water to surrounding lakes |
| **Reghed Glacier** | To the east |
| **Dwarven Valley** | Miles-long chasm from Kelvin's Cairn |

# Luskan

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Port City | **Status:** Damaged/Recovering | **Region:** Sword Coast North



---

## Overview

Luskan, the City of Sails, is a port city at the mouth of the River Mirar on the Sword Coast North. Once regarded as civilization's farthest reach, the city has suffered catastrophically. The Arcane Brotherhood's corruption, the Spellplague, and then a siege by the Black Sails company during Charles Vane's absence nearly destroyed it. Anyone sailing into its ports today sees nothing but wreckage — a city that is a shell of its former self.

---

## Geography

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Location** | Mouth of the River Mirar, Sword Coast North |
| **Layout** | Split north/south by the River Mirar; connected by Dalath's Span, Harbor Cross, and Upstream Span |
| **Size** | Large town (1480 DR) |
| **Population** | ~4,000 (1480 DR) |
| **Races** | Mostly humans, Drow |

### Northern Bank
Mostly warehouses. Includes the Mirabar District (fortified merchant compound), the Throat (main water tower), and Whitesails Harbor (Luskan navy only). Foreign ships dock at the unprotected Open Shore outside the walls.

### Southern Bank
Heavily fortified inner section. Home to most residents and shops. Features the Twin Teeth gate towers, the Captains' and High Captains' Court, the Market, the Prisoners' Carnival, and Dragon Beach — a lawless harbor where even soldiers fear to tread.

### Notable Islands
- **Host Tower Island** — The Host Tower of the Arcane; connected to both riverbanks
- **Closeguard Island** — Houses Ship Kurth; connected to the southern bank

---

## Commerce

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Imports** | Silver, scrimshaw, timber, dwarven weapons |
| **Exports** | Netherese Artifacts |

---

## Politics

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Government** | Oligarchy (1489 DR) |
| **Ruler** | Jarlaxle Baenre (1489 DR) |
| **Affiliations** | Formerly: Lords' Alliance; Formerly: Arcane Brotherhood |

---

## Campaign Notes

> [!warning] DM ONLY

## Overview

Menzoberranzan — the City of Spiders — is a large underground drow city-state in the Upper Northdark, possibly the most well-known drow city in all of Faerûn. It was the home of Drizzt Do'Urden, and many of his early adventures dealt with its political intrigue. Now, Drizzt's whereabouts are mostly unknown.

The city is 2 miles (3.2 km) below the surface of the Surbrin Valley, between the Moonwood and the Frost Hills. It is close to Mithral Hall and connected to it through tunnels. Other tunnels lead to the surface via the Moonwood and the western edge of the High Forest.

## Overview

The Mere of Dead Men (Elvish: *Merdelain* — "Slow Marching Court") is a cold saltwater swamp ~100 miles long by 30 miles wide along the Sword Coast North. ## Overview

Mirabar is a wealthy mining city on the Sword Coast, considered the richest city north of Waterdeep as of 1368 DR. 1,600 shield dwarves live underground near their workshops, producing metal goods for export; humans above handle mining operations, move ore to market, and defend against threats. body:
# Mount Hotenow

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Volcano | **Region:** Crags, Neverwinter Wood, northeast of Neverwinter | **Aliases:** Glaurimm (ancient dwarven name)

---

## Description (Public)
Mount Hotenow (pronounced *HOE-teh-NOW*) is a volcanic peak within the Crags mountain range, situated northeast of Neverwinter deep in Neverwinter Wood. It is home to a vast population of fire elementals, and their presence warms the Neverwinter River to the point where it never freezes — the source of the city's name. After its eruption, the peak resembles a set of serrated teeth, often described as "a door to the Nine Hells."

A river of pure elemental fire follows a serpentine path through Hotenow's interior and beneath the surrounding mountains of the Crags. The interior contains largely untapped deposits of alum, nitre, and sal ammoniac. Following the eruption, rare black diamonds have been found along the crater's rim.

**Shadowfell Counterpart:** A shadowy reflection of Mount Hotenow exists in the Shadowfell — unlike its Torilian counterpart, this version constantly spills rivers of lava that flow toward the city of Evernight.

---

## Description (DM Only)
> [!warning] DM ONLY
> - The Shadowfell reflection of Hotenow flowing toward Evernight is a detail worth developing if the campaign moves toward the Shadowfell. The volcano's fire-elemental population and Shadowfell echo make it a cosmologically significant site.
> - The black diamonds in the crater rim are potentially valuable — and guarded by the elementals and other dangerous inhabitants.
> - The Spree's occupation of Neverwinter gives them proximity to Hotenow. Consider whether they have attempted to exploit the volcano's resources.

---

## Key Landmarks
- **The Crater** — The volcanic peak itself; serrated and jagged after the eruption. Rare black diamonds found along the rim.
- **The Fire River** — A river of elemental fire that winds through the mountain's interior and beneath the surrounding Crags.
- **Neverwinter River Source** — The warmth from Hotenow's elementals heats the river below, keeping it ice-free through all seasons.

---

## Geography — Quick Reference
| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| **Type** | Volcano |
| **Location** | Crags range, northeast of Neverwinter, in Neverwinter Wood |
| **Aliases** | Glaurimm (dwarven); "door to the Nine Hells" |
| **Inhabitants** | Fire elementals, undead, dwarves, fire giants, red dragons, duergar |
| **Resources** | Alum, nitre, sal ammoniac, black diamonds (post-eruption) |
| **Shadowfell Echo** | Yes — perpetually erupting, lava flows toward Evernight |

---

## Connected Locations
- [[Neverwinter]] — Northeast of the city; Hotenow's heat keeps the Neverwinter River unfrozen
- [[Neverwinter Wood]] — The volcano sits within this forest
- [[The Shadowfell]] — Has a dark reflection here

---

## DM Notes
> [!note] Private
> - Primarily relevant as background lore for Neverwinter. Potential adventure site if the party needs to reach the Shadowfell via Hotenow's reflection, or if Spree-controlled Neverwinter begins mining the volcano.
> - The fire elementals are a natural defense — any expedition to the crater must contend with them.

# Myth Drannor

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Ancient Elven City | **Status:** Ruins (again) | **Region:** Cormanthor forest

---

## Overview

Myth Drannor — the City of Song — was the former capital of Cormanthyr, considered by many the pinnacle of shared knowledge, culture, and civilization in all of Faerûn. Tall, graceful stone towers; levitation pillars; skybridge walkways; streets wound between elven buildings and city parks. Defined by its ancient mythal.

For centuries it languished as fiend-infested ruins after the Weeping War. The elves of Evermeet reclaimed it in the late 14th century DR, sparking a century-long renaissance — before it fell to ruin once more.

---

## Architecture & Magic

The city was a wonder of magical engineering: levitation pillars, theurglass windows, Lifespring Wells (underground fresh water pumps), Dwarven Dungeons with air vent systems, and the Channels of the Earthborn Wind. The mythal's destruction left "Edgelands" in the surrounding forest — regions of leaked magical energy that still affect Cormanthor today.

The city's portals connected to: the Feywild, lower planes, demiplane of Dread, the planet Krynn, Eaerlann (via the Silversgate), and the Forest of Mir in Tethyr (via the Honorsgate).

---

# Neverwinter

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** City-State | **Faction:** Spree Alliance | **Status:** Fallen | **Region:** Sword Coast North



---

## Overview

Neverwinter — also known as the City of Skilled Hands and the Jewel of the North — was once regarded by Volo as the most cosmopolitan and civilized city in all of Faerûn. Renowned for its craftsfolk, precision water-clocks, multi-colored glass lamps, and magnificent gardens, the city once kept flowers blooming even through winter snow. It was a proud member of the Lords' Alliance.

That era is over. Following the fall of Helm's Hold and the collapse of the Knights of Legend, Neverwinter joined the Spree Alliance. Now it is ruled by Xissira, a dictator favored by the Spree, a far cry from its humble and just origins.

---

## Geography

| **Region** | Sword Coast North |
| **Location** | Northern Sword Coast, on the High Road between Waterdeep and Luskan |
| **River** | The Neverwinter River (heated by fire elementals beneath Mount Hotenow — never freezes) |
| **Size** | City (~1491 DR); formerly Metropolis (before 1451 DR) |

| **Imports** | Crafters, mercenaries |

| **Currency** | 1pp = 1 fairsail = 10 gold dragons = 20 electrum sea-shee = 100 silver bults = 1000 copper tharns |

---

## Districts

After the Mount Hotenow eruption of 1451 DR:
- **Protector's Enclave** — Central district; rebuilt over the old City Core
- **Blacklake District** — Northwestern section
- **Docks District** — Port and southern coastal area
- **River District (Towers District)** — Northeastern portion
- **Neverdeath Graveyard** — Main cemetery
- *(The Chasm, once replacing the old Arcanist Quarter, was sealed ~1485 DR)*

---

| **Current Ruler** | Xissira (Spree Alliance dictator) |

| **Affiliations** | Spree Alliance |

---

## Campaign Notes

> [!warning] DM ONLY
> **Status (1508 DR):** Neverwinter is firmly in Spree hands under Xissira. It is a cautionary tale for Lord's Alliance cities — once prosperous and just, now under dictatorial rule.
>
> The Neverwinter River still runs warm due to the fire elementals beneath Mount Hotenow. The city's famous craftsmanship continues, but its products now serve the Spree's war machine.
>
> The city is on the list of fallen cities — a data point that shows the players how far the Spree's reach has extended.

---

## Overview

Noanar's Hold is a small village just off the Evermoor Way on the edge of the High Forest, built around one of the most famous hunting lodges in the North. Cottages and well-kept stables ramble between the trees.

The Hunt Lords who once ruled it struck a deal with Orcus in their old age and were transformed into wights; their steeds became warhorse skeletons. # Orogoth

---

## Description (Public)
Orogoth is one of the most dangerous locations in the High Moor, primarily because of the black dragons that claim it as their domain. It is a large, sprawling ruined villa that once belonged to House Orogoth — formerly boasting great towers, which have long since collapsed.

Beneath the ruins, tunnels connect Orogoth to the **Serpent Tunnels of Najara**, the underground domain of the serpentfolk. This underground connection makes Orogoth a node between the surface ruins and something considerably more dangerous below.

---

> [!warning] DM ONLY
> - Orogoth's connection to the Serpent Tunnels of Najara makes it more than just a ruin — it's an entry point to a serpentfolk domain. This could be relevant if the campaign involves yuan-ti or serpentfolk plots.
> - The High Moor is southeast of the High Forest — geographically relevant to Chapter 4's theater. The presence of black dragons here is notable given Aurgolarossa's background.
> - Calathanorgoth is the named dragon ruler — could be a potential contact, rival, or source of draconic lore related to Aurgolarossa.

# Secomber

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Small Town | **Faction:** Lords' Alliance | **Region:** Savage Frontier / Western Heartlands border

---

## Overview

Secomber is a small, peaceful town that acts as the de facto border between the relatively peaceful Western Heartlands and the more savage North along the Sword Coast. Known for colorful gardens. It sits atop three stone hills at the junction of the Unicorn Run (flowing south) and the River Delimbiyr (flowing west).

South: the dangerous High Moor. North: Uluvin via the Secomber Trail.

---

## Key Facts

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Population** | ~1,417 (1372 DR) |
| **Races** | Primarily humans and halflings |
| **Ruler** | Lord Traskar Selarn |
| **Affiliations** | Lords' Alliance, Harpers, Order of the Gauntlet, Emerald Enclave |

---

## Related Notes

- [[High Forest]] — The Unicorn Run originates in the Star Mounts and flows south through here
- [[Daggerford]] — Down the Delimbiyr River

# Settlestone

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Village | **Region:** Fourthpeak, Frost Hills | **Affiliated with:** Mithral Hall

---

## Overview

Settlestone — originally called Dwarvendarrow — is a village in the southern spurs of the Spine of the World near Mithral Hall. Originally a dwarven settlement, it fell into ruins, then was resettled in 1358 DR by Icewind Dale barbarians under Berkthgar in alliance with Mithral Hall. Cold and windy, with mountains on three sides. Used by Mithral Hall as a public front and forward base.

---

## Key Facts

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Region** | Southern spurs of the Spine of the World, Fourthpeak |
| **Population** | ~400 (1358 DR) |
| **Races** | Dwarves, Reghedman humans |
| **Religion** | Tempus |
| **Allies** | Mithral Hall, Barbarians of Icewind Dale, Companions of the Hall |

---

## Related Notes

- [[Mithral Hall]] — Located nearby; Settlestone serves as its surface front
- [[Icewind Dale]] — Source of the barbarian re-settlers

name: Silverymoon
body:
# Silverymoon



## Quick Info
| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Region** | Luruar, Northwest Faerûn |
| **Type** | City — Major Settlement |
| **Controlling Faction** | [[The Fey Alliance]] (North Bank) — South Bank contested |
| **Status** | Occupied / Partially Destroyed |
| **First Visited** | TBD |
| **Access** | Open — with heavy Fey Alliance presence |

---

## Description (Public)
Known as the **Gem of the North**, Silverymoon is the prime signatory state of the Confederation of the Silver Marches and long considered the cultural heart of the North. It emulates the lost elven city of Myth Drannor in its architecture and traditions — a rare beacon of civilization in the untamed wilderness of the North.

The city is famous for its tradition of magic, its cultural life, and its welcome of all races of good intention. Even the occasional drow has found hospitality here. The permanent **Moonbridge** — a structure of pure magic — spans the River Rauvin and connects the city's two halves.

**Current State (1508 DR):** Silverymoon has been partially destroyed following the failed Spree invasion. The magical wards that protected the city were destroyed during Mourn Eveningfall's battle against Bane. The city is now under Fey Alliance control, with King Oberon ruling the North Bank. Lady Alustriel Silverhand, the former High Lady, remains in the city caring for the wounded Mourn Eveningfall.

---

## Description (DM Only)
> [!warning] DM ONLY
> - The destruction of Silverymoon's wards during Mourn's battle with Bane is directly tied to Aurgolarossa's long-term plan to dismantle divine infrastructure. The wards were partly powered by Helm's divine mandate — their destruction was not incidental.
> - Bryleth is operating in the background in Silverymoon and the surrounding High Forest. She is watching the party's movements.
> - The Wyrmworks survived the Spree invasion nearly untouched and served as a makeshift fortress. The owners (Roryk and Delgrath) may know more about dragon activity in the region than they let on — relevant to Aurgolarossa's history.
> - Alrue Crownshield, the Wyrmworks shop girl, is secretly an adult song dragon. This is a potentially significant contact if the party needs draconic knowledge or an ally against Aurgolarossa.
> - Lady Alustriel (daughter of Mystra, Chosen of Mystra) is here tending to Mourn. Her presence is both an asset and a target — Aurgolarossa's campaign against divine infrastructure makes powerful divine figures vulnerable.

---

## Key Landmarks
- **The High Palace** — Seat of government. Soaring spired castle of white marble on the eastern side of Northbank. Features the Silver Throne and Moon Seat. Has twelve underground levels including the High Mage's Vault and the Crypt of the High Mages.
- **The Moonbridge** — Magical bridge spanning the River Rauvin, connecting Northbank and Southbank. A permanent magical structure.
- **The House Invincible** — Major temple to Helm. Decommissioned as a full temple after recent events; previously served as headquarters for the Argent Legion.
- **The Golden Oak Inn** — Rustic inn on Dancer's Mask Lane, Northbank. Features a massive living oak tree through its center. Also serves as a temple to Shiallia. Favored by druids, rangers, and elves.
- **The Dancing Goat** — Bawdy tavern on the Moonway, Northbank. Known for revelry, pickpockets, and a secret door connecting to Harper Hall tunnels via subterranean passageways.
- **The Wyrmworks** — Dragon parts shop on Many Cats Lane, Northbank. Owned by Roryk and Delgrath. Survived the Spree invasion nearly intact, served as a makeshift fortress. Shop girl Alrue is secretly a song dragon.
- **Rauvinwatch Keep** — Fortified keep ~15 miles west of the city. Former headquarters of the Argent Legion under High Marshal Methrammar Aerasumé.
- **The Star Court** — Government building used for assemblies, citizen registration, and trade negotiations.
- **The Vault of the Sages** — TBD (see linked note)

---

## Current State
Silverymoon in 1508 DR is a city in recovery and occupation. The Spree's invasion failed militarily, but the destruction of the city's protective wards left it exposed. King Oberon and the Fey Alliance now control the North Bank, while the South Bank remains contested or in uncertain hands.

Lady Alustriel Silverhand remains in the city, caring for the gravely wounded Mourn Eveningfall after his battle with Bane and Lolth. The High Guard and Knights in Silver are diminished. The Argent Legion is scattered. The city's cultural institutions survive, but the political situation is fragile.

The party's current chapter is centered here and in the surrounding High Forest.

---

## History
Silverymoon was ruled for centuries by **High Lady Alustriel Silverhand**, one of the Seven Sisters, a Chosen of Mystra and powerful wizard. Under her leadership it became the beating heart of the Silver Marches.

The city endured the Spree's attempted invasion. During the battle, Mourn Eveningfall fought the god Bane directly — the clash destroyed the magical wards that had protected Silverymoon for centuries. Without its defenses, the city fell to Fey Alliance occupation. King Oberon now rules from the North Bank.

A **Tomb of the Unnamed Heroes** has been constructed northeast of Yartar (a related city) in memory of the adventurers who gave their lives in that city's liberation — a sign of how much the wider North has been reshaped by the Spree war.

---

## Key NPCs Present
- [[King Oberon]] — Rules the North Bank under Fey Alliance authority
- [[Queen Titania]] — True power of the Fey Alliance; present in the city
- [[Lady Alustriel Silverhand]] — Former High Lady, now caring for Mourn; Chosen of Mystra
- [[Mourn Eveningfall]] — Gravely wounded; recovering under Alustriel's care
- Roryk & Delgrath — Co-owners of The Wyrmworks; veteran adventurers
- Alrue Crownshield — Wyrmworks shop girl; secretly an adult song dragon

---

## Connected Factions
- [[The Fey Alliance]] — Currently controls North Bank
- [[Lord's Alliance]] — Founding member, now weakened here
- [[The Harpers]] — Have connections via the Dancing Goat tunnels
- [[Church of Helm]] — The House Invincible (decommissioned)

---

## Connected Events
- The Spree Invasion of Silverymoon
- Mourn Eveningfall's battle with Bane and Lolth
- Destruction of Silverymoon's magical wards

---

## Sessions Visited
- TBD

---

## Map Reference
*See existing Silverymoon map images in `zz_ Assets` — Northbank/Southbank layout, key landmarks labeled.*

---

## Rumors & Mysteries
- The secret door in The Dancing Goat supposedly connects to a network of passages leading to Harper Hall — where does it actually go now?
- The High Mage's Vault beneath the High Palace contains artifacts of extraordinary power. What happened to it during the invasion?
- The Crypt of the High Mages, six levels underground — are the entombed wizards still resting peacefully?
- Alrue Crownshield at The Wyrmworks is not who she appears to be.

---

## DM Notes
> [!note] Private
> - Silverymoon is the party's home base for Chapter 4. It should feel like a city under tense occupation — not a warzone, but not safe either.
> - The Fey Alliance's control here is a source of both resources and complications. Oberon wants things from the party; Titania has her own agenda.
> - The destruction of Helm's temple infrastructure (House Invincible, the wards) is part of Aurgolarossa's pattern — track it.
> - Consider what happens when Mourn recovers. Does he retake leadership of the city? Does that put him in conflict with Oberon?

### Geography — Quick Reference
| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| **River** | River Rauvin divides city |
| **North Bank** | Older section; High Palace, Market, Docks, most temples and noble houses |
| **South Bank** | Schools, Conclave of Silverymoon, caravan trade |
| **Currency** | 1pp = 1 unicorn = 10 gold dragons = 20 electrum swords = 100 silver shields = 1000 copper glints |
| **Demonym** | Silvaeren |
| **Races** | Humans 10%, Elves 30%, Half-elves 8%, Shield Dwarves 10%, Halflings 5%, Gnomes 2%, Other 35% |
| **Religions** | Mielikki, Oghma, Silvanus, Sune, Helm, Tymora (Mystra formerly; Cyric outlawed) |

body:
# Small Teeth

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Mountain Range | **Region:** Border of Amn and Muranndin | **Average Elevation:** 8,000 ft (2,000 m)

---

## Description (Public)
The Small Teeth are a cluster of mountains forming the border between Amn to the north and Muranndin to the south. Smaller and less imposing than many Faerûnian ranges, the Small Teeth average about 8,000 feet above sea level and are lightly forested except on their tallest peaks.

The mountains receive substantial snowfall; the resulting melt feeds the **Imnestream** and the **River Specie**, which flow through the Zehoarast Floodplains to the sea. The southern foothills are mild enough for viticulture — a good area for growing grapes.

The range's inhabitants include goblinoids, ogres, kobolds, and hill giants, making travel through the mountains hazardous for the unprepared.

---

## Description (DM Only)
> [!warning] DM ONLY
> - Reference geography for the Amn region. Distant from the current campaign theater.
> - Relevant if the party travels south to Amn or the Western Heartlands.

---

## Geography — Quick Reference

| **Region** | Border of Amn (north) and Muranndin (south) |
| **Elevation** | Average 8,000 ft (2,000 m) |
| **Vegetation** | Lightly forested; southern foothills suitable for grapes |
| **Rivers** | Imnestream and River Specie (snowmelt) → Zehoarast Floodplains |
| **Inhabitants** | Goblinoids, ogres, kobolds, hill giants |

---

> - Background/reference location. Only relevant if the campaign moves into the Amn/Muranndin region.

name: Spine Of The World
desc:
> [!info] At a Glance
> Type: Mountain Range | Region: Northwest Faerûn | Aliases: The Wall, Barakmornolor
body:
# Spine of the World

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Mountain Range | **Region:** Northwest Faerûn | **Aliases:** The Wall, Barakmornolor

---

## Description (Public)
The Spine of the World is a massive mountain range stretching across the northwest of Faerûn, from the Cold Run near the Sea of Moving Ice to the Ice Spires north of the Silver Marches. To those in Scornubel and the Savage Frontier, it is simply called **"the Wall"** — an apt name, as on winter days the snow-covered peaks blend with the iron-gray sky, appearing to hold up the heavens themselves. For most people, it simply marks the end of the known world.

The Spine shields the civilizations of the North from the freezing expanse of the Uttermost North — the Reghed Glacier and the Endless Ice Sea. Some peaks reach over 20,000 feet (6.1 km) in height; virtually all are covered in snow and ice year-round. The range experiences devastating icy winds and frequent rock slides. The western edge is penetrated by a single circuitous, always-dangerous trade route to Icewind Dale; otherwise the range is not approached by those without exceptional survival skills or very good reasons.

The northern slopes have enough trees for loggers from the Ten Towns to risk treks across the tundra to harvest. The southern slopes, near the Druarwood and Moonwood forests, are known as the **Lands Against the Wall**.

---

## Description (DM Only)
> [!warning] DM ONLY
> - The Spine of the World is primarily geographic context for the North — it defines why the Icewind Dale region is so isolated, and why travel to it is so dangerous.
> - The sole trade route through the Spine to Icewind Dale is a potential adventure location — a narrow, treacherous pass that various factions might want controlled.
> - The southern slopes near the Moonwood could be relevant if the campaign moves toward Kingdom of Many Arrows territory.

---

## Geography — Quick Reference
| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| **Type** | Mountain Range |
| **Region** | Northwest Faerûn |
| **Aliases** | The Wall; Barakmornolor |
| **Extent** | Cold Run (west) to Ice Spires (east), north of the Silver Marches |
| **Peak Height** | Up to 20,000 ft (6.1 km); all major peaks perpetually snow-covered |
| **Climate** | Subarctic; devastating icy winds, frequent rock slides |
| **Inhabitants** | Bugbears, drow, duergar, dwarves, fog giants, frost giants, goblins, hill giants, hobgoblins, humans, orcs, taer, verbeeg, white dragons, yeti, goliaths |
| **Notable Feature** | Single trade route through western edge to Icewind Dale |
| **Southern Slopes** | Lands Against the Wall; near Druarwood and Moonwood |

---

## Connected Locations
- [[Icewind Dale]] — To the north; accessible via the single pass through the Spine
- [[Kingdom of Many Arrows]] — Orc kingdom south of the Spine's eastern end
- [[Nether Mountains]] — Related mountain range further south
- [[Silver Marches]] — The Spine marks the northern edge of this confederation's territory

---

## DM Notes
> [!note] Private
> - Reference/geography location. The Spine defines the northern boundary of the campaign's primary theater.
> - If the party ever needs to reach Icewind Dale, crossing the Spine is its own adventure — blizzards, rock slides, monsters, and the single narrow pass to manage.

# Sundabar

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Large City | **Faction:** Lords' Alliance | **Region:** Silver Marches

---

## Overview

Sundabar — formerly Citadel Sundbarr — is one of the wealthiest and most militaristic cities in all of Faerûn. Originally a dwarven citadel, it evolved as its demographics changed. What never changed: it remains the rampart of the north. The Sundabarans are a vigilant, wary people. Outsiders and those who ask too many questions are viewed with suspicion.

---

# The Cloakwood

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Ancient Forest | **Region:** Sword Coast, south of Baldur's Gate

---

## Overview

The Cloakwood is an ancient, thickly grown forest on the south end of the Sword Coast in the Western Heartlands. The sages of Candlekeep assert it contains portals to several other parts of the cosmos — though these are believed defunct as of 1479 DR. Known for its many hiexel trees. The shoreline theoretically allows ships to moor and send boats ashore, but only desperate mariners face the forest's dangerous wildlife.

---

| **Region** | Sword Coast (south of Baldur's Gate) |
| **Inhabitants** | Fey, wild beasts |
| **Notable** | Portals (believed defunct as of 1479 DR) |

---

## Related Notes

- [[Baldur's Gate]] — City to the north
- [[Candlekeep]] — Scholars who study the forest's portals

body:
# The Evermoors

> [!info] At a Glance
> **Type:** Moorland | **Aliases:** Trollmoors | **Region:** Savage Frontier

---

## Overview

The Evermoors — also called the Trollmoors — is a region of the Savage Frontier comprising hills, troughs, chilly bogs, and rolling landscapes. Dense fog clouds the senses; winds roar constantly. Famous for its troll infestations. Despite the dangers, a few scattered drovers, shepherds, and prospectors manage to eke out a life within its borders.

In 932 DR, Nimoar and his barbarians eradicated all the trolls in the Evermoors and scorched much of the terrain. Scorch marks remained visible for hundreds of years afterward.

---

## Key Facts

| Field | Detail |
|-------|--------|
| **Region** | Savage Frontier |
| **Type** | Moor / bogland |
| **Inhabitants** | Bog blokes, ettins, hill giants, ogres, orcs, trolls |
| **Exports** | Copper, gold, lead |

## Climate

| Spring | 47°F / 8°C | 34 days | 17 days |

| Autumn | 46°F / 8°C | 16 days | 10 days |

name: Barovia
desc:
The valley of Barovia is the oldest and best known of the Domains of Dread. Originally a location in a forgotten world of the Prime Material plane, the entire valley was transported to the Shadowfell by mists controlled by evil entities known as the Dark Powers. The realm is a prison for its darklord, the vampire Strahd von Zarovich, as well as the entire population of the valley.
 
Almost the entire valley consists of densely forested rough terrain, with sheer cliffs and outcroppings. Few areas are easily traversable, and it is always dangerous to stay off the road. Winters are usually long and bitter.  
   
The sky of Barovia is permanently overcast with storm clouds. Even during the brightest hours of the day, light is unnaturally muted and dimmed, and do not affect normally sensitive creatures such as vampires. In many locations, the mists partially or completely obscure vision.
 
The valley is located between Mount Baratok to the north and Mount Ghakis to the south. Both mountains are part of a larger mountain range known as the Balinok Mountains. The evergreen forest of the Svalich Woods cover the entire valley, which is trespassed by the Old Svalich Road that runs from East to West. At the center of the valley, south of the slopes of Mount Baratok, stands a large lake known as Lake Zarovich, which is fed by melting snow from the mountain.  
   
It is bordered to the south by the domain of Hazlan.  
   
Government  
   
Barovia is ruled by Strahd, who is known by the population as "the devil Strahd". Most inhabitants are not aware that Strahd is a vampire. His ruling style is mostly through a proxy, as he makes few public appearances, and all the duties of tax collection are carried out by the villages' burgomasters. However, Strahd is a feared tyrant who takes whatever he wants and whom no one dare disobey.  
   
Defenses  
   
The valley is completely surrounded by deadly mists that sap the life force and can rapidly kill anyone who ventures inside. The mists are controlled by Strahd, who can choose to open or close the boundaries of his domain at will.  
   
Notable Locations

**Government**

His ruling style is mostly through a proxy, as he makes few public appearances, and all the duties of tax collection are carried out by the villages' burgomasters. However, Strahd is a feared tyrant who takes whatever he wants and whom no one dare disobey.  
   
**Defenses**  
   
The valley is completely surrounded by deadly mists that sap the life force and can rapidly kill anyone who ventures inside. The mists are controlled by Strahd, who can choose to open or close the boundaries of his domain at will.  
   
**Notable Locations**  
 

- The Amber Temple - an ancient temple built by an order of wizards that were later corrupted by the Dark Powers. The temple houses vestiges of entities from across the multiverse and is the place from which Strahd forged his original pact with the Dark Powers.
- Barovia, Vallaki, and Krezk - the three villages located along the Old Svalich Road.
- Castle Ravenloft - Strahd's seat of power.
- Oraşnou, a small village located at the foot of the mountains in Barovia. The mists lead there from the Quivering Forest.

   
**Inhabitants**  
   
Sometime in the late 15th century DR, the archmage Mordenkainen became trapped in Barovia. After an unsuccessful attempt to defeat Strahd and escape the realm, Mordenkainen went insane and lost his spellbook and staff. He roamed the base of Mount Baratok for over a year in search for a solution to his predicament before escaping to Waterdeep, whereby the Year of the Scarlet Witch, 1491 DR he was nursed back to sanity by Elminster and Storm Silverhand.

name: Bleak Academy
desc:
Founded by a trio of vampire masters, the Bleak Academy is a gothic school built in the Shadowfell focused on advancing the arcane and divine arts of necromancy. It was recreated from the pieces of the original site after being destroyed in the Material Plane due to its location above the legendary Tomb of Horrors, but since then it has seen quite a revival in faculty, students, and forbidden lore.  
The vampire masters formed the Tenebrous Cabal to help further their goals, and over the years they have attracted a large number of individuals with interests in the darker magical arts.  
   
The Bleak Academy is built from bone-white stone, but the vampires that control it can cloak the ground in a shadowy field to hide it from prying eyes. It also moves, though it’s unclear if this is just a natural part of the Shadowfell or if it’s a deliberate action by one or more of the Tenebrous Cabal leaders. Several towers spike up around the main building, which is shaped like a grinning skull, and in the center sits a planar observatory that only views the Negative Energy Plane. Intelligent undead of all kinds populate the halls, along with would-be necromancers, death domain clerics, and others with an interest and aptitude in necromantic magic.  
   
The library of the Bleak Academy is relatively small but contains the largest collection of dark tomes known in the Plane of Shadow. It is the prize of the Tenebrous Cabal, and one of the vampire masters can always be found within its book-laden chambers. Students and faculty are allowed to peruse the library’s collection, but visitors are not allowed to touch any of the books lest they risk the wrath of the Bleak Academy curse (which may be just a rumor, but so far has deterred most would-be thieves).

name: Burning Woods
desc:
The Burning Woods are a forest in the Shadowfell and a reflection of the Neverwinter Woods, a forest on the Prime Material world of Toril.  
   
Geography  
   
The woods stand not far beyond the eastern walls of the city of Evernight. The Shadowfell Road that begins in the city travel through this forest, beginning in a desolate stretch of wasteland with calf-deep mounds of gray ash and faintly glowing embers. Once it reaches the woods proper it becomes narrow forest paths, twisting and turning between the trees. Where those who travel without care could go miles in the wrong direction or end up where they began.  
   
Geographical Features  
   
Portions of this forest are swathed in extreme heat, pervasive smoke, or both due to the active volcano that stands within it. On occasion gouts of fire, lava, or small stones can be found raining from the sky due to it.  
   
Flora & Fauna  
   
The trees that fill this forest are blackened and glowing, always in a burning state since they were saplings. Some of these trees are sentient and quite angry, known to lash out at those who come near them. Burrowing undead can occasionally be found living within the heavy ash mounds of the forest, such as carcass eaters and corpse vampires. Bandits and other predators can be found lurking near its twisted forest paths, as they are the only truly usable roads.  
   
Notable Locations
body:
The Burning Woods are a forest in the Shadowfell and a reflection of the Neverwinter Woods, a forest on the Prime Material world of Toril.  
   
**Geography**  
   
The woods stand not far beyond the eastern walls of the city of Evernight. The Shadowfell Road that begins in the city travel through this forest, beginning in a desolate stretch of wasteland with calf-deep mounds of gray ash and faintly glowing embers. Once it reaches the woods proper it becomes narrow forest paths, twisting and turning between the trees. Where those who travel without care could go miles in the wrong direction or end up where they began.  
   
**Geographical Features**  
   
Portions of this forest are swathed in extreme heat, pervasive smoke, or both due to the active volcano that stands within it. On occasion gouts of fire, lava, or small stones can be found raining from the sky due to it.  
   
**Flora & Fauna**  
   
The trees that fill this forest are blackened and glowing, always in a burning state since they were saplings. Some of these trees are sentient and quite angry, known to lash out at those who come near them. Burrowing undead can occasionally be found living within the heavy ash mounds of the forest, such as carcass eaters and corpse vampires. Bandits and other predators can be found lurking near its twisted forest paths, as they are the only truly usable roads.  
   
**Notable Locations**  
 

- Den of the Hunters, a small earthen cave in the woods that is mostly protected from the occasional rains of fire. In 1479 DR, it was being used as a temporary home by a small group of bandits made up of mortal humanoids, shadar-kai, and "hunting hounds." These hounds were in fact humanoids that had been corrupted by the Shadowfell into having animalistic minds and deformed into running on all fours. Together they hunt the Shadowfell for mere sport.
- Mount Hotenow, the Shadowfell's reflection of a volcano of the same name on the Prime Material world of Toril. It is constantly spewing ash and cinder into the air. Rivers of magma flow down from it into various parts of the Burning Woods, which have fires dancing along their banks and emit thick gouts of smoke.

name: Domains Of Dread
desc:
The Domains of Dread, sometimes collectively known as the Demiplane of Dread or Ravenloft, are a series of demiplanes located in a remote corner of the Shadowfell and hidden from the rest of the plane by thick walls of mist.  
   
Geography  
   
The Demiplane of Dread is controlled by the almost completely unknown Dark Powers and sections of the demiplane are ruled over by dark lords. The Powers have no goals that anyone can confirm but seem attracted to people and creatures that commit evil acts or suffer from some terrible loss. They regularly inflict despair on the residents of the demiplane by simultaneously granting them boons and inflicting curses. Each curse is tailored to the individual and inflicts the greatest amount of emotional harm or distress upon them. This often spurs those individuals to commit greater acts of evil. It is apparent that the more heinous the deed, the greater the power and the worse the curse granted/inflicted upon the individual until a new land is generated on the demiplane and the individual is trapped forever within that land. These individuals become the Darklords, and they can never leave their domain. Others might be able to leave, but only if they have no great desire to, as the powers almost always intervene to dash the hopes of those trapped within their realm.

The Domains of Dread, sometimes collectively known as the Demiplane of Dread or Ravenloft, are a series of demiplanes located in a remote corner of the Shadowfell and hidden from the rest of the plane by thick walls of mist.  
   
Geography  
   
The Demiplane of Dread is controlled by the almost completely unknown Dark Powers and sections of the demiplane are ruled over by dark lords. The Powers have no goals that anyone can confirm but seem attracted to people and creatures that commit evil acts or suffer from some terrible loss. They regularly inflict despair on the residents of the demiplane by simultaneously granting them boons and inflicting curses. Each curse is tailored to the individual and inflicts the greatest amount of emotional harm or distress upon them. This often spurs those individuals to commit greater acts of evil. It is apparent that the more heinous the deed, the greater the power and the worse the curse granted/inflicted upon the individual until a new land is generated on the demiplane and the individual is trapped forever within that land. These individuals become the Darklords, and they can never leave their domain. Others might be able to leave, but only if they have no great desire to, as the powers almost always intervene to dash the hopes of those trapped within their realm.

- Aggarath - a domain contained within the hiltstone of a magical dagger, trapping the spirits of those slain by its blade. This was where Castle Spulzeer was transported when it disappeared from Toril.
- Arak - a domain ruled by drow until it disappeared in the Grand Conjunction.
- Barovia - the oldest and best known of the Domains of Dread. It was the domain of the vampire Count Strahd von Zarovich and had been visited by numerous inhabitants of the Realms, including Jander Sunstar as well as Minsc, Delina, Krydle, Shandie, a Kelemvorite cleric named Nerys and the famed explorer Volo.
- Bluetspur - a domain dominated by mind flayers.
- Burning Peaks - quite possibly the second best known of the Domains of Dread due to the fame of the two darklords who reside there, the Arch-Lich Vecna and his nemesis Kas the Betrayer.
- Darkon - the largest Domain of Dread, ruled over by the lich-king Azalin.
- Falkovnia - domain of the brutal and militaristic Vlad Drakov.
- Forlorn - the smallest of the Domains of Dread, and Jander Sunstar's place of residence after he left Barovia.
- Har'Akir - a desert domain ruled by the mummy lord Ankhtepot.
- Hazlan - the domain of Hazlik.
- I'Cath - formerly a kingdom in Kara-Tur and the domain of Tsien Chiang.
- Kartakass - the domain of Harkon Lukas after he fled from Barovia.
- Markovia - a wild and untamed domain populated with beast men, created from the crazed medical experiments of Frantisek Markov, the domain's beast lord.
- Monadhan – a jungle valley about a mile long and 2,500 feet wide at its widest point. It was once a simple jungle valley on the Material Plane that went by the same name. It is now lost to the Shadowfell.
- Saragoss - the domain of Draga Salt-Biter.
- Sri Raji - a jungle domain filled with dangerous tigers and ruled over by the rakshasa Arijani.
- Sunderheart – Known as the Funeral City. A place of escess and debauchery, haunted by deafening echoes of the past.
- Valachan - the domain of Urik von Kharkov. \> From \<[https://d.docs.live.net/0922afca00767e0d/Documents/World%20Locations%20Shadowfell.docx](https://d.docs.live.net/0922afca00767e0d/Documents/World%20Locations%20Shadowfell.docx)\>
